meetings.title: Workloads convo with MARSH (Matthew) and our PM Lead (Dana)
key_points: Customer discovery call with Matthew Elphick from MARSH to gather cloud workload requirements for Elisity's new workloads feature — part of ongoing punch list of questions collected from multiple customers.

Follow-ups from previous MARSH engagement reviewed: Ralph working through punch list with Matthew and Steve's team; one item still needs scheduling; another item Ralph confirmed should be complete in 2026.
MARSH has WCO in all phase two sites which will carry into phase three — approximately 580 sites now, targeting 2,600 by end of year on the new wireless plan/phase three policy.

MARSH multi-cloud environment: primary CSP is AWS (EC2 instances, VMs, OSS2 managed Kubernetes platform), Azure (Databricks, data pipelines), OCI (Oracle applications, backend VM systems), Oracle on AWS, and GCP (Apigee X for API/security API management).
Cloud vs on-prem ratio currently migrating toward 80% cloud / 20% on-prem within about a year; distribution roughly 60% AWS, 30% Azure, remainder OCI and GCP.

Dana began workloads discovery Q&A — asked about connectivity patterns between on-prem devices (already secured with Elisity) and cloud workloads, and most common traffic patterns (cloud-to-cloud vs on-prem-to-cloud).
Call cut short — Matthew received an AWS incident alert and had to leave to join an emergency call; meeting to be rescheduled to continue the workloads deep dive.
meetings.summary: Customer discovery call with Matthew Elphick from MARSH and PM Lead Dana Yanch to gather cloud workload requirements for Elisity's new workloads feature. Reviewed follow-ups from prior MARSH engagement (Ralph's punch list progress, WCO phase three rollout to 2,600 sites). Matthew detailed MARSH's multi-cloud environment: AWS primary (EC2, Kubernetes), Azure (Databricks), OCI (Oracle apps), GCP (Apigee X). Target 80/20 cloud-to-on-prem split. Dana started workloads discovery questionnaire but call was cut short when Matthew had to respond to an AWS incident. Meeting to be rescheduled.

action_items.description: Reschedule workloads deep-dive session with Matthew Elphick (MARSH) to continue discovery Q&A
Ensure Ralph completes outstanding punch list items with Matthew and Steve's team at MARSH
Compile MARSH cloud environment details (multi-CSP, workload types, traffic patterns) into workloads requirements doc

meetings.title: Workloads Sync Up
key_points: Follow-up from previous workloads architecture meeting — structured presentation of cloud workloads proposal for Elisity platform.
Proposal for two types of policy groups: device PGs (existing) and workload PGs with cloud-specific matching criteria (provider, tags, interface attributes).
Workload interfaces classified into PGs — most internal interfaces won't be important; focus on key production/management interfaces.
Hierarchical/nested policy group concept proposed by Chaku — sub-groups inherit matching criteria from parent groups for more granular workload segmentation.
Discussion of hardware support: 9500 switches, potential ASR support, and Palo Alto firewall integration considerations.
ITD propagation concerns for workload IP addresses across campus switches in large deployments.
Proposal kept intentionally simple with no additional dependencies — more advanced features can be added in future phases.
meetings.summary: Workloads architecture sync with Miha, Bill, and Chaku. Miha presented a structured proposal for cloud workload policy groups as a new PG type alongside existing device PGs, with matching criteria based on cloud provider, tags, and interface attributes. Chaku proposed a hierarchical PG model where sub-groups inherit parent matching criteria. Discussed hardware support scope (9500, ASR, Palo Alto) and ITD propagation challenges. The team agreed on a simple initial proposal with no extra dependencies.

action_items.description: Continue refining workload PG proposal with minimal dependencies for initial implementation
Evaluate hierarchical/nested policy group concept for workload segmentation
Determine supported hardware platforms for workload policy enforcement (9500, ASR, firewalls)

meetings.title: Aruba LPA Architecture Review
key_points: Discussion of MAB (MAC Authentication Bypass) behavior — whether to enable on AP interfaces, phone ports, and wireless connections; detection of device types to determine interface configuration.
Aruba policy structure deep dive: classes as reusable objects vs traditional ACLs, Radius role assignment, and how Aruba structures differ from Cisco.
LPA (Least Privilege Access) on legacy switches — how to push access lists to switches that don't support TrustSec/SGT.
Proposal: add LPA access list profile to policy group configuration — similar to how default security profile works today but for legacy enforcement.
Scenario walkthrough: device on TrustSec WLC gets matrix-based segmentation; same device on legacy switch gets ACL-based LPA profile pushed instead.
Discussion of whether LPA policies should be source-only or support destination-based/location-based policy sets.
Cedric raised question about Aruba class vs Radius role relationship — classes compose reusable matching objects that build into role policies.
meetings.summary: Architecture review of Aruba LPA (Least Privilege Access) for legacy switch support. Brent led the discussion on MAB behavior for different device types (APs, phones, wireless), Aruba policy structure (classes vs ACLs), and how to deliver segmentation on switches without TrustSec. Key proposal: add an LPA profile to policy group config so legacy switches get ACL-based enforcement while TrustSec switches use matrix-based segmentation. Paul contributed design patterns for PG-level LPA configuration. Cedric asked about Aruba class/role relationships.

action_items.description: Design LPA profile integration into policy group configuration alongside existing security profiles
Continue Aruba lab testing — resolve CoA issues and validate MAB authentication flows
Evaluate whether LPA policies need destination/location-based policy set support or source-only

slack_threads.summary: Extensive Aruba lab troubleshooting — CoA not working correctly, old GBP config conflicting with new policy, jump box disconnection during testing
slack_threads.relevance: Active lab testing directly supporting the LPA architecture being reviewed

meetings.title: Review Behavioral Identity Roadmap
key_points: Behavioral identity strategy framed around three core problem statements: unclassifiable devices, identity decay, and customer demand for better device clarity (e.g., Stanley Black & Decker).
Vision to use traffic analytics capabilities to enrich identity — explicitly not becoming NDR or threat detection; focus is on enriching identity as it exists today.
Phased go-to-market approach: enterprise CPS replacement first, then healthcare IoMT, then manufacturing — each requires specialized databases and protocol understanding.
Four anomaly detection use cases built by Sergey: DDoS attack, attacker/victim port scanning, and C2 — using isolation forest and Z-score ML mechanisms.
AI agent detection proposed as use case — detecting non-human identities behaving as AI agents, determining sanctioned vs non-sanctioned, registered vs non-registered.
CPS replacement positioning requires careful messaging — James aligned on strategic direction but must navigate existing CPS vendor partnerships (Armis valued at $7.7B sets high bar).
Pillar reordering agreed: CX-oriented use cases (device conformity, behavior gaps) promoted to A/B; advanced anomaly use cases moved to C/D.
Pavel's Jupyter notebook running against Cardinal Glass, MSK, and Halion customer data — Brent now has access to review anomaly detection results.
Common ML processing pipeline being built by Sergey — two teams now combining efforts into unified approach.
Non-human identity talk track identified as needed addition to the roadmap deck.
meetings.summary: Deep strategy session between Brent and Taylor reviewing the behavioral identity roadmap deck slide-by-slide before presenting to James. Defined three problem statements (unclassifiable devices, identity decay, customer demand for clarity) and a phased approach starting with enterprise CPS replacement. Reviewed four anomaly detection use cases using isolation forest and Z-score ML, discussed AI agent detection for non-human identities, and agreed to reorder pillars to lead with CX-oriented use cases. Sergey building common ML pipeline; Pavel's Jupyter notebooks running against customer data. Next step is another review before taking to James, with careful CPS partnership messaging.

action_items.description: Update behavioral identity roadmap slides with reordered pillars and refined CPS replacement messaging
Add dedicated slide for use case one (CPS replacement) and retool anomaly detection line of thought
Review and make sense of Pavel's Jupyter notebook anomaly detection results from Cardinal Glass, MSK, and Halion
Schedule follow-up review of behavioral identity deck before James presentation
Add non-human identity talk track to behavioral identity roadmap

meetings.title: LPA Aruba CX Deep-Dive (Miha & Jesse)
key_points: Miha built initial LPA RADIUS implementation for Aruba CX using CloudCode/Claude — code was largely complete at 48% context window, now polishing and fixing minor bugs.
Jesse walked through Aruba CX policy architecture in detail: IP classes (DHCP, DNS, RFC 1918), port access policies, roles, and how they chain together for enforcement.
Lab access plan: Jesse to set up Headscale/Tailscale VPN or jump box access so Miha can SSH directly to the Aruba 6300 switch for testing.
RADIUS accounting confirmed as the primary device tracking mechanism (replacing Syslog), with Aruba client tracking (IPDT equivalent) as a complementary discovery source.
COA vs disconnect clarified: Aruba requires disconnect (not COA) for re-authentication; Miha referenced Jacob's existing code patterns which Claude also discovered from GitHub PRs.

Port config discussed as the hardest part — multi-domain setup with client limits (max 5), IP phone detection, access point handling, and critical/failover/preauth roles for RADIUS server unreachability.
Custom lightweight RADIUS server implementation (not FreeRADIUS) — described as very lightweight, scalable, and stateless; multiple VEs can accept RADIUS access packets with only one sending COA.
Policy modification strategy: must modify policies in-place (not remove and re-add) to avoid orphaning devices on Aruba — uses temporary policy clone + COA migration approach.
meetings.summary: Deep technical session between Miha and Jesse on LPA RADIUS-based enforcement for Aruba CX. Miha has a working initial implementation built with Claude/CloudCode. Jesse walked through the full Aruba CX policy stack (classes, port access policies, roles) and lab setup. Key decisions: use RADIUS accounting (not Syslog) for tracking, disconnect (not COA) for re-auth, and in-place policy modification to avoid device orphaning. Lab access via Headscale/Tailscale VPN being set up so Miha can test against Jesse's Aruba 6300. Custom lightweight RADIUS server confirmed as the approach — stateless and scalable across multiple VEs.

action_items.description: Set up Headscale/Tailscale VPN or jump box access for Miha to reach Aruba 6300 lab switch
Complete initial LPA RADIUS testing against Aruba CX in Jesse's lab environment
Add RADIUS accounting support as next capability after basic enforcement is working
Determine wireless/access point handling strategy — whether to skip MAV config entirely for APs

Traffic analysis drawer slated for removal from matrix once CX confirms no impact. Meeting focused on reducing UI complexity for daily operational workflows.

meetings.title: Product Guardrails & UI Architecture Discussion
key_points: Referenced Prism FC's 'Device Insights' as a model: TAC team was unaware of outages until customers called in, highlighting the need for proactive guardrails and observability.
Chuck's 'YOLO' feature discussed — shows VEN switch capacity vs. active DAG entries, providing a policy guardrail for switch capacity limits.
Core UI problem identified: once a feature is added to the UI (views, filters, now tags), it is nearly impossible to remove — raising the stakes for any new UI addition.
Current complexity: customers already have views, have filters, and now tags are being introduced — the layering risks creating an unmanageable UI surface.
Agreement to build a clear document presenting 'this is the problem' with real-world customer environments before finalizing the tags/views/filters approach.
Need to present concrete examples showing what happens with the current approach vs. alternatives to get team alignment.
meetings.summary: Short two-person product architecture discussion on policy guardrails and UI complexity. Used Prism FC Device Insights and Chuck's VEN capacity feature as context. Central concern: layering views, filters, and tags creates irreversible UI complexity. Agreement to build a proper problem statement with real-world examples before proceeding, rather than continuing to add UI abstractions without a clear architecture rationale.

action_items.description: Build a clear document presenting the problem with views/filters/tags UI complexity, using real-world customer environment examples

ServiceNow integration confirmed as a 26.4 deliverable; Helion was the primary requesting customer, with one to two additional customers also interested.

Broad participation from engineering, product, UX, and CX-adjacent team members; Brent hosted in his personal meeting room.

ServiceNow integration confirmed for Helion (and 1-2 others). Policy matrix cell click navigation clarified.

Implement ServiceNow integration feature for 26.4 targeting Helion and other requesting customers
Implement 'finish action' at each editing workflow step based on CX team feedback

meetings.title: Elisity Roadmap Discussion with J&J
key_points: Grant (Head of Product) and Bill Pruitt (Head of Analytics) presented Elisity's 12-24 month product roadmap to Johnson & Johnson stakeholders.

Roadmap focused on four pillars: cloud and data center workload discovery and policy enforcement, AI-powered operations (progressing from AI-assisted to autonomous), AI agent detection capabilities, and ubiquitous edge visibility with device profiling.
Security posture assessment and segmentation scoring were highlighted as near-term deliverables.
J&J team expressed caution about autonomous AI operations in sensitive enterprise and OT environments, preferring AI-assisted with human oversight.
Non-human identity (NHI) integration between Elisity and J&J's IAM systems was discussed in detail, with Bishop (J&J) as the key contact for follow-up.
The discussion covered both IT and OT (Operational Technology) security use cases within J&J's environment.
J&J team provided feature requests and expressed interest in deeper dive sessions on specific roadmap topics.
meetings.summary: Elisity product roadmap presentation to Johnson & Johnson. Grant (Head of Product) and Bill Pruitt (Head of Analytics) walked J&J stakeholders through Elisity's 12-24 month vision, covering cloud workload discovery, AI-driven security operations, NHI identity integration, and device profiling. J&J expressed strong interest but flagged caution around fully autonomous AI in sensitive environments. Follow-up actions center on NHI/IAM integration with J&J's identity systems and scheduling deeper technical dives on areas of interest.

action_items.description: Schedule follow-up conversation about non-human identity (NHI) integration between Elisity and J&J's IAM systems
Discuss and document feature requests raised by J&J during the roadmap session
Coordinate deeper dive technical sessions on specific roadmap topics of interest for J&J

meetings.title: Policy Tags/Mocks/Prototypes
key_points: Jacquie demonstrated a working prototype for policy tag management, allowing creation, storage, and hierarchical organization of tags — built over the weekend.
Tags are intended to be applied to policy groups (current sprint scope) and eventually to workloads, enabling richer enforcement matching by interface attributes.

The concept of 'workload groups' was introduced as distinct from policy groups: policy groups define criteria for assets, while workload groups categorize workloads by business function (e.g., prod vs. DevOps) rather than device identity.

Significant confusion emerged among participants about the overall architecture — how tags, policy groups, workload groups, and workload types relate to each other — indicating the vision has not been clearly communicated.
The team discussed tagging interfaces on workloads (e.g., AWS SGT mappings) so Elisity can match traffic and apply enforcement; this is a new capability not yet fully defined.
Real-world customer examples (Cardinal Glass, Helion) were proposed as anchors for mocking up use cases to make the abstract concepts more concrete.
Dana flagged that without a unified diagram showing how all components connect, participants cannot effectively contribute to or validate the design.
Paul left early due to a conflicting Johnson & Johnson meeting; the meeting ended with agreement to schedule separate focused sessions on architecture and use cases.
meetings.summary: Working session to review Jacquie's weekend-built prototype for policy tag management. Jacquie walked through the UI for creating and organizing tags hierarchically, with planned integration into policy groups and eventually workloads. The discussion exposed significant alignment gaps: participants were unclear on how tags, workload groups, workload types, and policy groups interrelate. Dana and Paul pushed for a clearer architectural diagram and real-world customer examples to ground the design. The meeting concluded without resolution, with action items to schedule dedicated sessions for architecture alignment and to create visualization artifacts.

action_items.description: Schedule a dedicated architecture session to align on overall vision, problem statements, and how tags/policy groups/workload groups interconnect
Create a diagram showing how all components (tags, policy groups, workload groups, workload types, interfaces) map together
Mock up real-world customer examples (Cardinal Glass, Helion) to demonstrate tag management functionality in context
Schedule separate session focused specifically on future workload implementation and how it differs from current policy groups

meetings.title: Elisity - Catalyst Center InterOp conversation
key_points: Catalyst Center versions 2.3.5+ introduced two additional DNS flow monitors per interface, consuming all four available flow monitor slots and leaving no room for Elisity's ingress/egress monitors — the root cause of the conflict.

A key config discrepancy was identified: Elisity's flow records are missing the 'match application name' field required for AVC mode, which would otherwise allow multiple monitors on the same interface.

Elisity's go-forward fix involves two virtual edge behavior changes: retaining global NetFlow config when no physical interfaces are programmed, and updating the VE to process Catalyst Center's flow record format (initiator/responder fields) instead of requiring its own source/destination records.

Elisity's support documentation for Catalyst Center coexistence was written for the AVC-only config, not the newer four-monitor DNS behavior, meaning the published guidance does not apply to CHOP's current environment.
Even if the AVC mismatch were corrected, the four-monitor limit imposed by Catalyst Center's current behavior means Elisity and Catalyst Center cannot coexist on the same interfaces today.

An engineering special or early release was requested by CHOP but Jesse advised against it, citing QA risks for network-managing software; only a service-impacting P1 scenario would justify bypassing normal release cycles.

Catalyst Center's compliance engine was flagged as a risk: any config inserted outside its templates will be overwritten; the recommended mitigation is using Catalyst Center templating and excluding Elisity's security policy commands from compliance checks.

As a temporary workaround, CHOP agreed to exempt a small number of Jolly Lab switches from Catalyst Center management, accepting degraded compliance and limited operational capability (no code upgrades, no pre/post checks) as a short-term trade-off.

Maine campus — representing ~65% of CHOP's infrastructure including all APs and switches — must remain on Catalyst Center telemetry; losing Maine flow data would effectively eliminate meaningful network visibility across the environment.

Disabling Elisity flow collection on a switch is a per-switch toggle in the Elisity console; historical NetFlow data is retained for ~30 days, but new analytics needed for enforcement policy modeling would stop accumulating.

Wireless controllers (WLCs) are also affected, though multiple flow monitor profiles are supported via CLI on newer IOS-XE code; Catalyst Center manages WLC config through programming interfaces, not the web UI.

In the end-state solution, Catalyst Center will be the source of truth for device configuration and flow collection, while Elisity will act as a passive observer — owning only security policy elements such as SGACLs and SXP mappings.
Release containing both fixes is targeted for end of April, with customer upgrades beginning early May; CHOP can be prioritized to the earliest upgrade cycle given the criticality.

A follow-up call was agreed upon for the next day, including Amar, Steven, John, and Rob, to determine the minimum requirements needed to keep the enforcement program progressing while the fix is in development.
meetings.summary: Deep technical session on the Catalyst Center / Elisity NetFlow coexistence conflict at CHOP. Root cause confirmed: Catalyst Center 2.3.5+ consumes all four available flow monitor slots per interface with DNS monitors, blocking Elisity's monitors. Two VE fixes were scoped (retain global config, process CC flow record format); release targeted for end of April with CHOP prioritized for earliest upgrade. Temporary workaround agreed: exempt Jolly Lab switches from Catalyst Center while Maine campus stays on Catalyst Center telemetry. End-state architecture positions Catalyst Center as source of truth for flow collection, with Elisity owning only SGACLs and SXP policy elements.

meetings.title: Customer Deployment & Roadmap Session
key_points: New 9250 switch stacks need to be deployed at production floors as soon as possible to cover operating rooms, community care, and lab areas before discovery and policy enforcement begins.
Consistency risk flagged: if discovery and rules start at some sites while other sites with existing devices remain undeployed, policy enforcement will be inconsistent across the organization.
Even without full site coverage at Bridgewood/Ridgewood, enough density exists to capture the majority of devices and begin device audit classification.
Enforcement can proceed per-site and per-switch once devices are discovered and classified, with trust scoring tracked over time — enforcement doesn't need to happen at the site level simultaneously.

Infusion pumps and Echo devices cited as concrete examples of policy-based communication rules — devices need to talk to specific servers (e.g., dynamic server for Echo), and those rules replicate automatically when new sites are onboarded.
Group communication policies will define which device types can communicate with which groups, with the ability to shut off unnecessary inter-group communication and refine granularity over time.
Brent presented the Elisity product roadmap: identity-driven zero trust security for every device, user, and workload, with emphasis on simplified deployment and operations.

New capabilities highlighted: AI-driven device classification using private LLM (not public API calls), Elisity Assistant with natural language interaction added in latest release, monthly platform releases with new identity graph integrations (Assemble connector recently added).
Data plane expansion continues: support for Cisco 9350 and new Juniper switch models added in latest release, with ongoing vendor additions planned.
Cisco licensing clarified: DNA Essentials supports discovery and analytics but enforcement requires DNA Advantage or Network Advantage; customer is in process of replacing 3850s within three months.
Customer confirmed plans to migrate to Epic Cloud over the next two years, moving a large portion of applications — this will significantly change network topology and traffic patterns.

AI model data privacy discussed: Elisity uses single-tenant SaaS architecture with no cross-customer data sharing today; model currently trained only on internal documentation, with potential for anonymized cross-customer learning in the future.

Customer expressed strong interest in cross-hospital shared learning for AI models to avoid retraining after cloud migration, seeing value in understanding traffic patterns from other healthcare environments.
AI agent detection on the network discussed as emerging use case — identifying unauthorized AI agents, applying strict controls or quarantine based on whether they're on company-owned systems.
Weekly meeting reduced from one hour to 30 minutes as breakout sessions are introduced; first breakout session for 400 Market Street location scheduled for Friday.
meetings.summary: Customer weekly deployment meeting combining operational status with a product roadmap presentation. First half covered switch deployment priorities (9250 stacks for production floors), device discovery consistency across sites, and per-site enforcement strategy. Second half featured Brent's roadmap pitch covering AI-driven classification, Elisity Assistant, cloud workload discovery, and data plane expansion. Customer engaged deeply on AI model privacy and cross-hospital shared learning, and confirmed Epic Cloud migration plans over the next two years.

meetings.title: CHOP Deployment Planning - Teams Session
key_points: New 9250 chassis and switch stacks need to be deployed on production floors (operating rooms, community care, lab) as soon as possible
Device discovery should cover all production floor switches before policy rules are created to ensure consistency across sites
Policy group (PG) matching fidelity improves with more devices discovered and classified — enforcement can happen per-switch level
Enforcement approach discussed: start per-site, then expand — can achieve enforcement once device tracking establishes trust tags
Device audit classification is critical — more devices classified means higher-fidelity rules and better policy matching
Risk identified: if rules are created before all devices are discovered, inconsistencies will occur across sites like Bridgewood and Ridgewood
meetings.summary: CHOP deployment planning session focused on new switch stack rollout across production floors (operating rooms, community care, labs). Discussed device discovery strategy — need comprehensive discovery before creating enforcement rules to avoid inconsistency. Agreed enforcement can start per-switch and expand per-site, with trust tags building over time as devices are tracked and classified.

meetings.title: Cloud Workloads Architecture Sync
key_points: Phase 1 scope is intentionally limited — importing AWS data and onboarding initial workloads — with Kubernetes and more complex scenarios deferred to later phases.
Core architectural agreement reached: a workload is a distinct object from a device, consists of multiple interfaces, can belong to multiple groups, and may require different policies per interface.
SGT-based enforcement tied to IP-to-interface mapping is the only current enforcement capability, making interface-level IP assignment the critical dependency for campus-to-cloud policy.

Interface as an attribute vs. a separate object was debated; consensus leaned toward treating the interface as an attribute of the workload to avoid splitting workloads and to address existing customer complaints about unlinked interfaces.
Concern raised that 60-80% of customers may resemble Licity — no campus-to-cloud traffic, fully internet-facing workloads — meaning the enforcement use case may not apply to the majority.

Three customer cloud topology buckets identified: static campus-to-EC2 (clear enforcement target for Phase 1), purely cloud-native with no campus traffic (no enforcement needed), and hybrid behind load balancers (more complex, deferred).
GSK cited as a concrete example — 20,000 VMs and 4,450 pods today, trending toward managed Kubernetes, though actual migration pace may lag their stated plans.
Customer vertical matters significantly: healthcare customers (e.g., Providence) are mostly on-prem and slow to adopt cloud, while others like Marsh are actively requesting this capability.

Chuck proposed a two-matrix model: one for campus policy groups using SGT, and a separate one for cloud/Kubernetes workloads using a different enforcement method, with interface objects attached to workloads.
Child policies concept discussed as a potential mechanism to support workload grouping and SGT assignment, but confirmed to be on hold with no current agreement.
CD Projekt Red flagged as a strong Phase 1 candidate — heavily automated workloads, campus-to-cloud traffic, AWS-based, and a known contact available to potentially share data.
Scale is a key concern — GSK's 20,000 workloads would generate a large number of static mappings, raising questions about DZ design; a dedicated workload DZ was proposed.
meetings.summary: Architecture sync focused on extending Elisity's policy enforcement from campus to cloud workloads (primarily AWS). Phase 1 will focus on static campus-to-EC2 enforcement with Kubernetes deferred; key debates covered workload vs device modeling, interface attributes, and a two-matrix policy approach.

meetings.title: Account Strategy & Roadmap Sync
key_points: 35 first meetings tracked since early February, signaling strong pipeline momentum but raising the question of which deals are genuinely viable.
GSK presentation went well, though Brent acknowledged roughly 50% of it was aspirational — committed areas are workload discovery and North-South policy, not lateral movement control.
Lateral movement control was flagged as a fundamentally different product requiring support across cloud providers, on-prem data centers, bare metal, VMs, and hypervisors.
GSK remains a credible target given the established relationship and credit built up; focusing on the container space was seen as a realistic, simplified story.
UBS was assessed as too complex to pursue seriously — massive scale, heterogeneous network, every cloud and VM type — making it a poor fit given current product maturity.
EBS follow-up with James was discussed; the current stance is to invite him back if he has a concrete idea, otherwise the opportunity is effectively parked.
Renewal risk flagged for an existing account — only deployed in the OT environment with an all-you-can-eat license they won't fully utilize in the near term.
A roadmap session of roughly one hour was agreed upon to arm the security team with collateral and insight ahead of their internal whiteboard session against IT.
Ongoing tension between the security team and IT team at the account, with IT pushing Cisco and Palo Alto while security is advocating for the current solution.
Brent will use the same roadmap presentation delivered at GSK, covering both workload and AI topics, to support the security team's position.
meetings.summary: Quick strategy sync reviewing pipeline health (35 first meetings since Feb), assessing key accounts (GSK positive, UBS too complex, EBS parked), and planning a roadmap session to support a customer's security team in an internal competitive battle against IT.

meetings.title: CHOP Escalation Prep Session
key_points: CHOP's escalation is driven by internal politics — friction between three misaligned groups: Amar (implementation), Stephen/John (IT operations), and Dave Kerr (solutions architecture).

Root cause: CHOP notified the vendor of their DNA Center usage in September, sent configs showing an older code version, but later upgraded to 2.3.5 and enabled additional flow behavior without engaging Cisco or the vendor.
Technical finding: in version 2.3.5, network services analytics cannot be selectively disabled; enabling it produces a different config that omits Source and Destination IP values.
John, the network operations lead, independently started using flows in Catalyst Center for troubleshooting and monitoring without coordinating with the vendor.
Two damaging perceptions to counter on the call: that the vendor knew about the problem and ignored it, and that only CHOP is actively working on a resolution.
Testing with Evolving confirmed that adding the vendor's exporter to the DNAC Global Flow Monitor did receive flows, but parsing failed due to different field headers.
Planned 2064 behavior change: leave global Netflow config on the switch, set all interfaces to manually disabled, allow CHOP to add the vendor's exporter to their flow monitor.
A published article by Taylor Caldwell is being cited by CHOP as evidence of a gap; the article is accurate for older configs but no longer reflects current Catalyst Center behavior.
Broader impact: Orlando Health and at least five or six other customers are expected to start using Catalyst Center flows, making this fix a wider priority.
Presentation structure agreed for the afternoon CAP call: Pete builds the background and timeline summary, Jesse adds slides on the planned fix.
meetings.summary: Preparation session for the CHOP escalation call, driven by internal politics and a technical misalignment after CHOP upgraded to Catalyst Center 2.3.5 without coordination. Team aligned on root cause narrative, a planned fix (2064 behavior change), and presentation structure for the afternoon CAP call.

meetings.title: ELT Weekly Leadership Sync
key_points: RSA event exceeded expectations — strong cross-pollination between existing customers and prospects, with Minoz confirming interest in returning next year.
Palantir is running McKinsey-style top-down AI engagements with hospital CEOs and boards, locking out CISOs from platform visibility.
Q1 revenue tracking closer to $4.5M vs. a $4.9M target, with 4-5 deals needing to close; five PVs done in March with four more needed in April.
Pipeline concentration risk is a key concern for both Q1 and Q2.
Stage two-and-a-half is identified as the core funnel problem: qualified prospects without full budget or decision-maker readiness falling into a black hole after AE handoff.
Huntington kickoff completed — projected to be the largest deployment ever at 1,500 sites; also marks the company's first entry into the financials vertical.
GSK EBC ran four and a half hours — only 30 minutes on current state, with four hours focused on AI and workflow strategy.
Brent's AI pitch was well-received at GSK, but the scale of positioning, CX, deployment, content, and training requirements around AI is seen as overwhelming.
Shannon's feedback from the EBC was positive — Mike noted the partnership has produced results but acknowledged significant work remains.
Final vendor decision between Palo Alto and Zscaler for the remaining SASE platform will be presented to Shoby next Monday, with Palo Alto expected to win.
St. Luke's updated commercials are being finalized; Glenn's procurement involvement is raising trust concerns.
Tender offer paperwork is being finalized and will go out via Arista to existing accounts within seven days.
All-hands agenda for Tuesday next week to include a readout on Napa and RSA.
Natalie selected as Employee of the Month.
meetings.summary: Executive leadership weekly sync covering RSA success, Q1 revenue tracking ($4.5M vs $4.9M target), pipeline conversion bottleneck at stage 2.5, Huntington's record 1,500-site deployment kickoff, GSK EBC results, and tender offer timeline.

meetings.title: Brent Colwell, Elisity's Personal Meeting Room
meetings.summary: Extended personal meeting room session with Reada and Jacob. Full notes not available from Krisp.

meetings.title: LPA Technical Options Review
key_points: Go-to-market discipline flagged as a core concern — without strict controls, introducing LPA risks an unmanageable influx of customer requests.
Brent confirmed no viable political pathway with Aruba over the next 12 months, making the RADIUS-based LPA approach the only technical solution.
Two distinct vendor buckets identified for Meraki: SMB/retail (not a fit) vs. enterprise deployments where Cisco runs Catalyst at hospitals and Meraki at clinics.
LPA framed as a macro segmentation stopgap — customers would receive basic L3/L4 policy enforcement at the edge while upgrading toward micro-segmentation.
Meraki flagged as technically limited — minimal ACEs per switch (roughly 250), no Netflow support.
RADIUS-based policy association explained as more scalable than downloadable ACLs.
Wireless explicitly scoped out — LPA focus is wired-only.
RADIUS server placement still undecided — options include a dedicated RADIUS server or embedding it as a service within the VE.
Interoperability variability acknowledged as a risk — HP alone runs three different underlying OSes; agreed to limit initial scope to CX platforms.
Engineering handoff plan outlined — Brent to transfer all exploratory findings and lab access to the Tiger team (R&D).
Go-to-market communication strategy deferred — agreed not to announce a change in strategy, but rather frame it as adding Aruba support.
Agreed to start with enterprise-grade Aruba CX platforms, with $2-3M in potential ARR at Cinquora as the primary unlock target.
meetings.summary: Technical deep-dive on the LPA (Lightweight Policy Agent) approach for expanding switch vendor support beyond Cisco. Decided on RADIUS-based enforcement starting with Aruba CX as the first target, with $2-3M ARR at stake. Engineering handoff to R&D Tiger team planned.
